title: Tier-1 features must signal when they degrade to Tier-0 behavior
scenario: recordLesson(--quality-gate) called callDspy() which returned null when the DSPy service was unreachable or returned 500. The lesson was still persisted (graceful degradation) but no user-facing WARN was emitted, so users who opted into --quality-gate got a false sense of safety during DSPy outages. Discovered while writing adversarial fallback-chain tests for issue #14 (PR A #17). Reproduction: stand up a local DSPy stub in mode 'timeout' or 'score:500', call recordLesson with dspy.enabled=true, observe persisted=true + qualityScore=null + zero warnings anywhere.
wrongApproach: Treat a null return from the Tier-1 enrichment call as 'nothing to do' and continue silently. The code read `if (evalResult) { ... persist with score } // else fall through and persist anyway` — no else branch, no WARN, no signal. This pattern is seductive because it's a clean fallback, but it violates the Progressive Enhancement contract: Tier-1 opt-in features must be observably ON or observably OFF, never silently-on-then-silently-off.
correctApproach: Add an explicit else branch: when dspy.enabled=true AND callDspy() returns null, write a WARN to process.stderr before persisting. Use process.stderr.write (not console.warn) so the message has no Node-version-specific prefix and is trivially assertable in subprocess tests. Contract: `⚠  [quality-gate] DSPy evaluation skipped: service unavailable. Lesson persisted WITHOUT quality check.` Same pattern applies to src/cli/eval.ts — force useDspy=true then emit `⚠  DSPy unavailable: semantic evaluation skipped. Results reflect L1+L2 only.` after the guard runs if dspyEval was null.
insight: Every opt-in Tier-1 feature (DSPy quality gate, DSPy semantic eval, federation provider, any future external-service integration) must have a user-visible signal when it degrades to Tier-0. 'Graceful degradation' without signaling is silent degradation — a false guarantee. Write subprocess tests asserting the signal appears at the stderr boundary, plus a negative-control test with a healthy stub asserting the signal does NOT appear. The negative control cannot share an event loop with the stub: use spawn (async) not spawnSync, otherwise the parent's event loop blocks and the stub never accepts the subprocess's connection.

title: When fixing a bug pattern, grep the entire codebase before declaring the contract closed
scenario: PR #20 (issue #19) closed the silent-degradation contract for two call sites — recordLesson(--quality-gate) and `eval <file>` — and recorded án lệ L-2026-04-29-silent-tier1-degradation. The contract was declared complete. Post-merge audit (forced by maintainer challenge "CI xanh là test xanh, nhưng logic đúng chưa?") surfaced two more sites with the exact same bug pattern: searchLessons() in --semantic mode silently fell back to string matching when callDspyRank() returned null, and the default `verify` flow with useDspy=true emitted no signal when DSPy was unavailable because the hollow-artifact guard's Check 4 had no else branch for null dspyEval. Same shape, two more sites, both passed CI green. The pattern is: a Tier-1 enrichment call returns null on failure, the code treats null as "nothing to do" and falls through to Tier-0 behavior with no user signal.
wrongApproach: Fix the bugs at the two sites named in the issue, ship a PR with green CI, declare the contract closed. The implicit assumption is that if the issue named two sites, those are the only two sites. The corollary failure mode is that the first án lệ documented the contract abstractly ("every Tier-1 feature must signal degradation") but did not enumerate every Tier-1 site, so the lesson was unactionable for a future executor scanning the codebase. CI green misled into believing logic-completeness; tests assert what they assert, not what they could have asserted.
correctApproach: When you discover a bug pattern (not just a bug instance), treat the discovery as a contract definition, not a point fix. Step 1: name the pattern in one sentence ("Tier-1 silent degradation: any opt-in enrichment call whose null return is silently swallowed"). Step 2: grep the codebase for every site that matches the pattern signature — for this codebase: `grep -n "callDspy|callDspyRank|provider.resolve" src/` plus a manual review of every opt-in feature's failure path. Step 3: enumerate the matched sites in the issue or án lệ so future executors know the count. Step 4: fix all sites in one PR, or open a follow-up issue with the remaining sites listed by file:line. Step 5: write at least one adversarial test per site with a negative control to prove the signal is gated, not always-on. Recording this meta-lesson in the án lệ makes the contract enforceable rather than aspirational.
insight: Pattern coverage is a separate concern from instance correctness. A PR can fix every bug it claims to fix and still leave the contract violated at undiscovered sites. The first án lệ from issue #19 stated the contract correctly but did not protect against this exact failure mode — there was no procedural step requiring the executor to enumerate every site of the pattern before declaring the contract closed. This is itself runtime evidence for issues #22/#23: institutional memory only compounds value if lessons capture procedural rules, not just abstract principles. A lesson that says "emit a WARN when Tier-1 degrades" is half a lesson; the other half is "and grep for every site of this pattern before declaring done."
